entries.title: Engagement tools overview - a set of Pro utilities under the right-click menu
entries.body:
# Engagement tools - overview

**Engagement tools** are a set of helper utilities in the **Professional** edition that are not placed in a separate tab but live in the **context menu**. This is their main feature: they have no "home" screen in the row with **Proxy**, **Repeater**, **Intruder**. To reach them, you need to right-click somewhere and choose the **Engagement tools** submenu.

## Where they are invoked

The **Engagement tools** submenu appears in the context menu almost everywhere Burp shows requests, responses, or application nodes:

- in **Target > Site map** (on a host, a branch of the tree, or a specific URL);
- in **Proxy > HTTP history**;
- in the **message editor** - including on selected text in a request/response;
- in other places that have request tables.

The set of items in the submenu depends slightly on **where** you clicked from. For example, it makes sense to invoke **Analyze target** on a host in the site map, and **Find references** on a selected fragment in the editor.

## What the set includes

Under the single umbrella of **Engagement tools** are gathered things that differ in purpose:

- **Find references** - searches across all Burp tools for responses that **reference** the current item (where in the application this URL/resource appears). Helps understand where links to a page come from.
- **Analyze target** (**Target analyzer**) - assesses the scope of work on the target: how many dynamic and static URLs there are, how many parameters, and what their names are.
- **Discover content** (**Content discovery**) - an active search for hidden, unlinked content (directories, files) that is not in the visible navigation.
- **Generate CSRF PoC** - generates an HTML page that reproduces the selected request from the victim's browser (a CSRF demonstration).
- **Simulate manual testing** (**Manual testing simulator**) - a generator of "background" traffic resembling manual testing (a niche utility).
- **Schedule task** - scheduling tasks (for example, a scan) to run on a schedule.

> In this reference, **Target analyzer**, **Content discovery**, **Generate CSRF PoC**, and **Manual testing simulator** are covered in detail. **Find references** and **Schedule task** are mentioned for completeness.

## Why all this is needed

The idea of **Engagement tools** is to cover small but recurring pentest needs right inside Burp, without switching to third-party software:

- **gauge the scale** of the target before diving in (**Analyze target**);
- **dig down to hidden** content that is not visible via links (**Discover content**);
- **quickly assemble a working PoC** to confirm CSRF (**Generate CSRF PoC**);
- **find all occurrences** of a specific resource across the tools (**Find references**).

## Edition

**Engagement tools** are **Professional** functionality. In **Community Edition** this submenu is absent: in the free edition you will find neither **Analyze target**, nor **Discover content**, nor the CSRF generator. This is one of the notable arguments in favor of Pro for practical work.

## A tip from practice

Get used to right-clicking **in the right place**. Many who have only just moved to Pro look for these tools in the tabs and do not find them - because there is no tab. Memorize the chain: **right-click > Engagement tools > the utility you need**. And remember the context: the set of items changes depending on what exactly you clicked (a host in the site map, a history row, or selected text in the editor).

entries.title: Target analyzer (Analyze target) - assessing the scope of work on the target
entries.body:
# Target analyzer - assessing the scale of the target

**Target analyzer** (the **Analyze target** menu item) is a utility that, based on the content already gathered in the site map, calculates **how large and complex** the target in front of you is, and helps decide where to direct your effort. It is not a vulnerability scanner: it attacks nothing, it merely sums up quantitatively what Burp has already seen.

## How to launch it

1. Open **Target > Site map**.
2. Select one or more **hosts** or the branch of the tree you want to assess.
3. Right-click and choose **Engagement tools > Analyze target**.

A separate window with a report on the selected part of the application will open.

## What it counts

**Target analyzer** parses the captured content and produces summary metrics:

- the **total number of dynamic URLs** (pages that accept parameters);
- the **total number of static URLs** (without parameters);
- the **total number of parameters**;
- the **number of unique parameter names**.

These figures are a rough but honest measure of effort: an application with hundreds of dynamic URLs and dozens of unique parameters requires a completely different plan than a dozen static pages.

## The report tabs

The report window is split into four tabs:

- **Summary** - aggregated counters (dynamic/static URLs, parameters, unique names). It also offers the ability to **save the report as HTML**.
- **Dynamic URLs** - a list of URLs with parameters; for each you can see a request/response preview and the set of parameters.
- **Static URLs** - a list of URLs without parameters with the full request/response.
- **Parameters** - a breakdown by parameter: for each name it shows **in how many URLs** it occurs, with the ability to "drill down" to specific URLs.

The **Parameters** tab is especially useful: it quickly surfaces parameters that "roam" across many endpoints - exactly the ones most often worth checking first.

## An important limitation

The analyzer looks **only at what is already in the site map**. It does not crawl the application itself and does not extend coverage. If the site map is incomplete, the figures will be understated and the picture distorted.

The practical takeaway: **first populate the site map** (manual browsing through the proxy, a scanner crawl, and if necessary **Discover content**), and only then run **Analyze target**. Otherwise you are assessing not the application but the stub of it that Burp happened to see.

## A practical scenario

You have been handed a broad scope (several hosts), and you need to gauge what to tackle:

```
1. Прогнать каждый хост через браузер/краул, чтобы наполнить Site map.
2. Target > Site map -> выделить хосты -> Engagement tools > Analyze target.
3. На вкладке Summary сравнить хосты по числу динамических URL и параметров:
   где больше динамики -> там больше поверхности атаки.
4. На вкладке Parameters найти параметры, повторяющиеся во многих URL,
   -> это первые кандидаты на проверку инъекций/IDOR.
5. Сохранить отчёт (Save report) для записей и планирования.
```

## A tip

Use **Target analyzer** as a **planning** tool, not a bug-finding one. Its job is to answer the question "how much work is here and where to start" so that you consciously allocate your time across a large scope rather than drowning in the first host you come across. And remember: the quality of the report depends directly on the completeness of the site map - garbage in, garbage out.

entries.title: Content discovery (Discover content) - finding hidden, unlinked content
entries.body:
# Content discovery - digging up hidden content

**Content discovery** (the **Discover content** menu item) is an active tool for finding **content and functionality that the application does not link to**. The kind of thing that is not visible during ordinary browsing and that the scanner's crawler will not pull in: forgotten admin panels, backups, service directories, old versions of files. This is targeted name brute-forcing, not vulnerability scanning.

## How to launch it

1. Select any HTTP request (in **Site map**, **Proxy > HTTP history**, and so on).
2. Right-click > **Engagement tools > Discover content**.
3. In the window that opens, click the **Session is not running** button to start a discovery session (the button's label reflects the current state of the session).

Each session is tied to a starting point (a directory) from which Burp begins its digging.

## What techniques it applies

**Content discovery** combines several approaches at once:

- **name guessing** - brute-forcing through dictionaries of file and directory names;
- **web crawling** - following found links to expand coverage;
- **extrapolation from the target's naming conventions** - Burp notices **how** the application names resources and builds out similar ones (for example, from `AnnualReport2018` it tries `AnnualReport2019`).

The search is **recursive**: a directory is found - Burp creates subtasks under it and digs deeper, trying new names and variants inside it.

## The Control tab

**Control** is the session's real-time console. Here you see the progress of the work:

- **Requests made** - how many requests have been sent;
- **Bytes transferred** - how much data has been transferred;
- **Network errors** - the number of network errors;
- queues: discovery tasks, spider requests, responses awaiting analysis.

A toggle button **pauses and resumes** the session. The **Queued tasks** table shows prioritized discovery operations - and it expands recursively as new directories are found.

## The Config tab

**Config** is where the brute-forcing logic is configured. The key blocks:

- **Target** - the starting point and coverage: **Start directory** (the initial path) and **Discover** (search for files, directories, or both; optionally, the subdirectory depth).
- **Filenames & extensions** - dictionaries: built-in short/long lists or your own; the **Names observed in use on target site** option (use names actually encountered on the target) and **Derivations based on discovered items** (algorithmic derivations from what was found).
- **Discovery engine** - the engine's behavior: case handling (auto/forced), **Add discovered content to suite site map** (add findings to the main site map), **Spider from discovered content** (run an ordinary crawl from what was found), **Number of discovery threads** (the number of threads - regulates the load).

## The Site map tab

Results are stored in this session's **separate site map** (the **Site map** tab), where you can see all the content found within the coverage. Findings can be **exported to Burp's main site map** so you can work with them further using the other tools (send to **Repeater**, scan, and so on).

## How it differs from the scanner's crawl

These are different tasks. **Content discovery** is a narrowly specialized **brute-force of unlinked** content: it deliberately guesses what there are no links to. **Burp Scanner**, when crawling, follows **visible links** and looks for vulnerabilities along the way. Roughly: the scanner walks what is **visible**; **Discover content** digs down to what is **hidden**.

## A practical scenario

```
1. Нашли каталог приложения, напр. https://target/app/.
2. Правый клик по запросу -> Engagement tools > Discover content.
3. Config: Start directory = /app/, Discover = files and directories,
   включить Names observed... и Derivations..., поднять словарь до длинного.
4. Нажать Session is not running -> сессия пошла.
5. Следить на вкладке Control: Requests made, Network errors, очереди.
6. На вкладке Site map просматривать находки; интересное (напр. /app/backup/,
   /app/admin-old/) -> выгрузить в основную карту сайта -> в Repeater.
```

## Tips

- **Regulate the threads.** Brute-forcing that is too aggressive makes noise in the target's logs and loads the server - on production, reduce **Number of discovery threads**.
- **Enable extrapolation.** The **Names observed...** and **Derivations...** options often yield the most valuable findings: Burp adapts to the specific application's naming rather than blindly hammering a generic dictionary.
- **Export findings to the main site map**, otherwise they will stay "locked" in the session's site map and will not be picked up by Burp's other tools.

entries.title: Generate CSRF PoC - generating an HTML exploit for CSRF
entries.body:
# Generate CSRF PoC - assembling a PoC page for CSRF

**Generate CSRF PoC** is a utility that, from a selected HTTP request, assembles an **HTML page** that reproduces this request from the victim's browser. By opening such a page, an authenticated user inadvertently repeats the target action - and that is the clear demonstration of a **CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)** vulnerability.

## How to open it

1. Select the URL or HTTP request you need (in **Site map**, **HTTP history**, **Repeater**, and so on).
2. Right-click > **Engagement tools > Generate CSRF PoC**.

The generator window with two panes will open.

## The two panes of the window

- **The top pane** - the full HTTP request you selected. It can be **edited right here**: change parameters, headers, the method - tailor it to the attack scenario you need.
- **The bottom pane** - the generated **HTML code** of the PoC, which in the browser will repeat the request from the top pane.

## The main buttons

- **Regenerate** - rebuild the HTML after edits to the request above. Changed something in the top pane - click **Regenerate** so the bottom one updates.
- **Test in browser** - test the PoC in Burp's built-in browser: it produces a unique URL with the current HTML payload. Open it and watch the requests in the **Proxy** to make sure the attack really works.
- **Copy HTML** - copy the generated code to use it externally (paste into a report, host it on your own stand, and so on).

## The Options menu

The **Options** button opens the generation settings:

- **CSRF technique** - the way the PoC is formed: **Auto** (the recommended auto-selection) or a manual choice of technique.
- **Include auto-submit script** - if enabled, JavaScript is added to the HTML that **submits** the request **by itself** when the page loads (the victim does not need to click anything).

## How different requests are handled

The generator takes the request type into account. For bodies in **XML** or **JSON** it applies either form encoding or a cross-domain **XmlHttpRequest** - depending on which has a chance to work. If the content type or method are such that the PoC may not execute in the browser, Burp shows a **warning**: this means the exploit most likely will not work in a real browser, and the scenario needs to be reconsidered.

## A practical scenario

Suppose that in **Repeater** you confirmed that the email-change request goes through without an anti-CSRF token:

```
1. Правый клик по этому запросу -> Engagement tools > Generate CSRF PoC.
2. В верхней панели проверить/поправить тело: новый email атакующего.
3. Options: CSRF technique = Auto, Include auto-submit script = вкл.
4. Regenerate -> в нижней панели готовый HTML.
5. Test in browser -> открыть выданный URL под активной сессией жертвы.
6. В Proxy убедиться, что запрос ушёл и email сменился -> CSRF подтверждён.
7. Copy HTML -> вложить PoC в отчёт.
```

## Tips

- **Simplify the request first.** In the top pane, remove extra headers and parameters that do not affect the action - the PoC will become cleaner and more robust in the browser.
- **Do not ignore the warnings.** If Burp says the method/content type is problematic, do not attach such a PoC to a report without checking it with **Test in browser**: a "non-working" exploit devalues the finding.
- **Auto-submit is for persuasiveness.** An enabled **Include auto-submit script** shows that the victim is not required to do anything beyond opening the page - this strengthens the demonstration of impact.

entries.title: Manual testing simulator (Simulate manual testing) - a background traffic generator
entries.body:
# Manual testing simulator - simulating manual testing

**Manual testing simulator** (the **Simulate manual testing** menu item) is the most niche of the **Engagement tools** utilities. It generates traffic **resembling a manual pentest**: it sends typical test payloads to random URLs and parameters at irregular intervals, so that from the outside it looks like the work of a live tester.

## What it does (and does NOT do)

The simulator takes an area selected in the site map and sends requests across it, imitating the "handwriting" of manual testing - irregular intervals, different payloads, different points. But the key point is honestly recorded in the documentation: **Burp does not process the responses in any way**. That is, **you will not find a single vulnerability this way** - plausible traffic goes outward, but there is no analysis of the results.

In essence this is a **noise generator**, not a bug-finding tool. In the documentation it is presented with a touch of irony - as a semi-easter-egg from the creators: a way to "create the appearance of active work" while you are busy with something else.

## How to turn it on and off

1. Select the part of **Target > Site map** the imitation will run over.
2. Right-click > **Engagement tools > Simulate manual testing**.
3. Control is via a single checkbox, **Simulation running**: check it - it starts, clear it - it stops.

## Settings

There are practically no settings: the documentation describes only the on/off toggle (**Simulation running**). No dictionaries, intensity, or payload choice can be configured - the tool is deliberately minimalist.

## Honest take: when this is ever useful

Let us be frank: in real vulnerability hunting the **Manual testing simulator is not needed**. It scans nothing and finds nothing. Possible (and rather exotic) uses:

- **Demo/training** - to show what manual-testing traffic looks like in logs, in an IDS/WAF, in a SIEM (for example, so a blue team can practice recognizing patterns).
- **Detection testing** - to see how defensive tools react to "human-like" irregular traffic with typical payloads.
- **Filling pauses** - to create the appearance of activity on a stand (that very ironic scenario from the docs).

For actual pentesting, use **Scanner**, **Intruder**, and **Repeater** - and keep this menu item in mind more as a curious fact about Burp's arsenal.

## A tip

Do not confuse **Simulate manual testing** with real testing tools. If your goal is to find a vulnerability, this is **not the** menu item. Run the simulator consciously and only for a specific task about **traffic** (a demonstration, a monitoring check), not about **finding bugs** - and do not forget to clear the **Simulation running** checkbox so that extra noise does not go to a live target.
